title: Saint Antony: Life, Finding, and Translation
summary: CME00090 is the checked-in repository copy of the University of Michigan encoding of Karl Horstmann's 1881 edition of a three-part Middle English prose compilation about Antony the Great. The encoding retains the complete medieval text on printed pp. 116-138 but omits Horstmann's manuscript description and dialect study on pp. 109-115. Horstmann transcribed the sole direct witness, British Library Royal MS 17 C XVII, fols. 124v-133r. The current BL component record separates the compilation into a partial English version of the Athanasius-Evagrius life (BHL 609; fols. 124v-126r), the legendary finding and first translation of the relics to Constantinople (BHL 612; fols. 126v-131v), and the second translation into France/Vienne (BHL 613; fols. 132r-133r); medical recipes begin on fol. 133v. A complete 648-page scan of Anglia 4, the complete BHL reference volume, the complete Analecta Bollandiana volume containing BHL 612, and the complete Acta Sanctorum volume containing BHL 613 are cached locally. The BL catalog lists no digitised content for Royal MS 17 C XVII, so no public complete manuscript facsimile is claimed; the record preserves physical-request and reference-enquiry routes and asks for complete whole-object imaging separately from the work's target folios.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00090
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00090
entities.description: A public electronic text of Horstmann's complete edited Middle English compilation, printed pp. 116-138. The encoding retains three DIV2 parts, page and folio milestones, notes and italic typography while excluding the article introduction on pp. 109-115.

entities.label: Karl Horstmann, S. Antonius (vita, inventio, translatio) (1881)
entities.description: Horstmann's Anglia article gives a manuscript contents description and dialect study on pp. 109-115, followed by his edition of the three Middle English prose components on pp. 116-138. He describes Royal 17 C XVII as damaged by damp, faded and written in one hand, and tentatively infers that the Johannes named elsewhere in the codex was the scribe-compiler.

bibliographic.citation: Karl Horstmann, 'Prosalegenden. V. S. Antonius (vita, inventio, translatio),' Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 4 (1881), 109-138.
bibliographic.title: Prosalegenden. V. S. Antonius (vita, inventio, translatio)

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Anglia volume 4
entities.description: A 648-page PDF scan of the complete serial volume. Horstmann's full article is PDF pp. 121-150; the CME00090 medieval-text selection is PDF pp. 128-150.

entities.label: British Library Royal MS 17 C XVII
entities.description: An early-fifteenth-century paper miscellany in English and Latin, with tenth-century vellum flyleaves. The Saint Antony compilation occupies fols. 124v-133r; the three components begin at 124v, 126v and 132r, and medical recipes begin at 133v.

notes: The current catalog describes gatherings chiefly of sixteen leaves and the English as Northern (?).
Horstmann reported 162 leaves in the manuscript's then-current state, severe damp damage and fading, and one hand across the codex; the current BL record reports 166 leaves.
The vellum flyleaves are remnants of a tenth-century English Latin lectionary.

entities.label: Vita Antonii in Evagrius's Latin translation (BHL 609)
entities.description: The Latin translation by Evagrius of Antioch of Athanasius of Alexandria's Greek Life of Antony. Royal 17 C XVII component 20a is a Middle English version of the opening portion, corresponding to chapters 1-13 in the Patrologia Latina printing cited by the BL catalog; the exact Latin exemplar used by the Middle English translator is unknown.

entities.label: Finding and first translation of Saint Antony to Constantinople (BHL 612)
entities.description: The anonymous or traditionally attributed Latin legendary account of Antony's hidden body, its discovery by Bishop Theophilus and its first translation to Constantinople. The Middle English rubric associates the account with Theophilus, but this is not treated as verified historical authorship.

entities.label: Second translation of Saint Antony into Gaul (BHL 613)
entities.description: The Latin legendary account of the relics' movement from Constantinople into the Vienne region, the Montmajour association and the development of Antony's healing cult. It is the source work of the third Middle English component; the exact Latin exemplar remains unidentified.

entities.label: Bibliotheca Hagiographica Latina volume 1 (1898)
entities.description: The standard Bollandist reference volume whose Saint Antony entries distinguish BHL 609, 612 and 613 and cite their principal printings.

bibliographic.citation: Socii Bollandiani, Bibliotheca Hagiographica Latina Antiquae et Mediae Aetatis, vol. 1 (Brussels: Société des Bollandistes, 1898).
bibliographic.title: Bibliotheca Hagiographica Latina Antiquae et Mediae Aetatis

entities.label: Analecta Bollandiana edition of BHL 612 (1883)
entities.description: The complete Latin text headed 'Incipit translatio sancti Anthonii confessoris,' printed from Namur manuscript 159 on pp. 341-354 of Analecta Bollandiana volume 2. The opening and conclusion correspond closely to Middle English part II.

bibliographic.citation: 'De S. Antonio Abbate: Incipit translatio sancti Anthonii confessoris,' Analecta Bollandiana 2 (1883), 341-354.
bibliographic.title: De S. Antonio Abbate

entities.label: Acta Sanctorum edition of BHL 613 (1863)
entities.description: The Latin translation history from an old Utrecht manuscript printed under 17 January on pp. 515-516 of the third-edition January volume 2. Its prologue and narrative correspond to Middle English part III.

bibliographic.citation: Acta Sanctorum, Januarii II, 3rd ed. (Paris and Rome: Victor Palmé, 1863), 515-516, 'Eadem historia ex veteri MS. Ultrajectino.'
bibliographic.title: Translatio S. Antonii Magni Abbatis

agents.name: Evagrius of Antioch
notes: Translator of Athanasius's Greek Life of Antony into Latin; not the Middle English translator.

agents.name: Anonymous Latin hagiographer
notes: Conventional agent for BHL 612 and 613; it does not assert common authorship.

scope.notes: Identifier and content match; exact transfer date and intervening normalization remain undocumented.

subject.description: Horstmann's complete article inside the complete volume scan

scope.notes: Printed pp. 122 and 135 are shared boundaries between adjacent parts. Horstmann's folio milestones and the current BL component starts support the ranges; the next codex item begins at 133v.

object.description: Opening of Evagrius's Latin translation of Athanasius
locators: chapters 1-13 in the BL catalog's PL cross-reference

scope.notes: This identifies the source work, not the unidentified physical Latin exemplar. The manuscript rubric's Jerome attribution is preserved as a source-layer conflict.

subject.description: Middle English finding and first-translation component

scope.notes: This identifies the source work, not a particular surviving Latin codex.
This identifies the source work, not a particular surviving Latin codex.
The edition prints a Latin witness from Namur manuscript 159; it is supporting source evidence, not an upstream source used by the fifteenth-century translator.
The edition prints an old Utrecht manuscript; it is supporting source evidence, not an upstream source used by the fifteenth-century translator.

access.access_method: Open the exact BL Archives and Manuscripts record.
Use the BL collection-item request form with shelfmark Royal MS 17 C XVII. The record requires a letter of introduction and consultation in London; Reader Pass rules apply.

No item-specific digital-content link or complete public image sequence was present in the current exact catalog record. Ask BL Reference Services whether complete whole-object imaging can be supplied.

rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English and Latin textual content
rights.basis: The textual works and witness were created centuries before modern copyright. This does not govern modern photographs, transcriptions, editions, interfaces or provider terms.

rights.component: U-M CME digital text and item materials
rights.basis: The U-M CME collection applies the DPLA No Copyright-United States statement to its public materials.
notes: This jurisdiction-specific statement must not be generalized to BL manuscript photographs or other countries.

rights.component: Horstmann's 1881 article, transcription and editorial apparatus
rights.basis: The article was published in 1881, well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.

rights.component: 1898 BHL volume text and reference entries
rights.basis: Published in 1898, before the current United States public-domain cutoff.
Published in 1883, before the current United States public-domain cutoff.

rights.component: 1863 Acta Sanctorum edition and editorial matter
rights.basis: Published in 1863, before the current United States public-domain cutoff.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and access derivatives
rights.basis: The underlying 1881 volume is public domain in the United States, but the item supplies no explicit worldwide reuse license for the scan files.

rights.component: Any future British Library manuscript photographs or supplied digital facsimile
rights.basis: No item-specific public image object or license was available to inspect, and no reproduction has been supplied. Publication, redistribution and commercial-use permission must be established from the terms attached to any future delivery.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A 166-folio miscellany with many grammatical, devotional, literary and medical items
editorial_practices.representation: Manuscript description followed by an edition only of the Saint Antony compilation on fols. 124v-133r
editorial_practices.description: Horstmann's article selects the three-part Antony unit from the larger codex; it is not an edition or facsimile of the entire manuscript.

editorial_practices.representation: Only the edited Middle English text on printed pp. 116-138
editorial_practices.description: The encoding begins with the article's medieval rubric and omits Horstmann's title, contents survey, source discussion and dialect study on pp. 109-115.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Horstmann's italic type used for expansions, supplied letters, Latin and German editorial matter
editorial_practices.description: The XML preserves printed italic styling but a single HI element does not identify the original manuscript abbreviation or distinguish expansion from every other editorial use of italics. The manuscript or facsimile is needed to recover original signs.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The long finding rubric appears before part I and is repeated before part II
editorial_practices.representation: Horstmann prints it as the compilation heading and again at part II
editorial_practices.description: Horstmann argues that the rubric properly belongs to the inventio component rather than to the preceding life. The manifest preserves the witnessed placement and the editor's scope correction separately.
editorial_practices.scope: Opening rubric and part II heading

open_questions.question: Did one translator or compiler assemble all three Middle English components, and was that person Johannes Presbyter/Purvare?
next_steps: Consult modern codicological and paleographical scholarship on Royal MS 17 C XVII.
Compare translation technique across the three components.
Keep Horstmann's identification tentative rather than turning shared hand and an elsewhere colophon into certain authorship.

open_questions.question: How should current dialectology reconcile the BL catalog's Northern (?) description with Horstmann's mixed Midland and tentative western localization?
next_steps: Check eLALME/LALME and later manuscript-specific studies.
Keep scribal dialect distinct from translation or composition place.
Preserve both dated assessments until a modern profile is verified.

open_questions.question: Can the British Library provide a complete color facsimile of Royal MS 17 C XVII and written reuse terms?
next_steps: Request covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges if offered, and every manuscript leaf rather than only fols. 124v-133r.
Map the work portion separately to fols. 124v-133r and its three component ranges.
Ask explicitly about Git redistribution and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand use before publishing supplied images.

evidence.summary: On 2026-07-12 the exact official record contained no digitised-content link, and exact shelfmark searches found no separate public complete facsimile. This is a dated negative finding, not proof that no internal digitization exists; the BL reference form is retained for enquiry.

evidence.citation: Socii Bollandiani, Bibliotheca Hagiographica Latina Antiquae et Mediae Aetatis, vol. 1 (Brussels, 1898), entries 609, 612 and 613.
evidence.summary: Standard scholarly reference distinguishing the Athanasius-Evagrius life and the two relic-translation narratives and citing their historical editions; the complete volume PDF was downloaded and verified.

evidence.citation: 'De S. Antonio Abbate: Incipit translatio sancti Anthonii confessoris,' Analecta Bollandiana 2 (1883), 341-354.
evidence.summary: Complete Latin BHL 612 text from Namur manuscript 159 and complete-volume scan, matching the Middle English part II's opening, narrative sequence and conclusion.

evidence.citation: Acta Sanctorum, Januarii II, 3rd edition (Paris and Rome, 1863), 515-516, 'Eadem historia ex veteri MS. Ultrajectino.'
evidence.summary: Latin BHL 613 text and commentary matching the Middle English part III's prologue, translation into Vienne, Montmajour connection and healing-cult material; complete volume downloaded and verified.

evidence.citation: United States Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.summary: Official overview supporting the United States public-domain status of works published in 1881, 1883, 1898 and 1863; provider terms and other jurisdictions remain separate.
Defines the jurisdiction-limited public-domain assertion and warns that other rights or other countries may differ.

review_notes: The intellectual unit is a witness-specific three-part Middle English compilation, not Horstmann's complete article or an undifferentiated life of Antony.
All manuscript-to-Latin relations identify abstract source works; none claims that the anonymous Middle English translator used the later Bollandist printings or any identified surviving Latin codex.
The manuscript rubric's Jerome attribution, Horstmann's compiler inference and competing dialect localizations remain attributed and qualified rather than silently normalized.
Every public supporting PDF used was cached complete and hard-linked to the main checkout; the manuscript remains uncached because no complete public facsimile was verified.
